[[Kayal Iro]] (son) }} }} '''Kevin Leslie Iro'''[http://nzleague.co.nz/kiwis/player_profile.php?letter=I&id=604 IRO, KEVIN LESLIE 1987 - 98 - KIWI #604] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120402171207/http://nzleague.co.nz/kiwis/player_profile.php?letter=I&id=604 |date=2 April 2012 }} ''nzleague.co.nz'' (born 24 May 1968) is a former professional [[rugby league]] footballer and coach who played in the 1980s, 1990s and 2000s, and [[Coach (sport)|coach]]ed in the 2000s. A {{rlp|wg}} or {{rlp|ce}}, he played for [[Mount Albert Lions|Mount Albert]], [[Manly Warringah Sea Eagles|Manly-Warringah]], [[Hunter Mariners]] and the [[New Zealand Warriors|Auckland Warriors]], and in England for [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]], [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] and [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] at club level, and played in [[Challenge Cup]] finals for all three English teams. Internationally, he played 34 times for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] and later played 3 times for the [[Cook Islands national rugby league team|Cook Islands]]. He also played for [[Auckland rugby league team|Auckland]] and the [[Rest of the World rugby league team|Rest of the World]]. He was nicknamed "The Beast". He coached the Cook Islands from 2005 to 2006. ==Background== Iro was born in [[Auckland]], New Zealand, and is of [[Cook Islands]] descent. Iro is the younger brother of fellow [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] international [[Tony Iro]], and is the father of [[Kayal Iro]], who is also a professional rugby league footballer. ==Playing career== ===Early career=== As a junior, he played for the [[Glen Innes, New Zealand|Glen Innes]] Falcons, a feeder club to [[Ellerslie Eagles|Ellerslie]] during the 1980s.[http://www.ellerslieleague.co.nz/?page_id=22 History] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130208033739/http://www.ellerslieleague.co.nz/?page_id=22 |date= 8 February 2013}} ''ellerslieleague.co.nz'' ===Wigan=== Kevin Iro played at {{rlp|ce}}, and scored two [[Try (rugby)|tries]] and three [[Try (rugby)|conversion]]s in [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]]'s 22–17 victory over [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] in the [[1988 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1988–89 Rugby Football League season|1988–89 season]] at [[Knowsley Road]], [[St Helens, Merseyside|St. Helens]] on Sunday 23 October 1988,{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2745|title=1988–1989 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-date=13 July 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180713170348/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2745|url-status=dead}} Kevin Iro played at {{rlp|ce}} and scored a try in Wigan's 12–6 victory over [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]] in the [[1988–89 League Cup (rugby league)|1988–89 John Player Special Trophy]] Final,{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2756|title=7th January 1989: Wigan 12 Widnes 6 (John Player Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-date=18 May 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150518082424/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2756|url-status=dead}} and played at {{rlp|ce}} in the 12–6 victory over [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]] in the [[1989–90 League Cup (rugby league)|1989–90 Regal Trophy]] Final during the [[1989–90 Rugby Football League season|1989–90 season]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]], [[Leeds]] on Saturday 13 January 1990.{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2840|title=13th January 1990: Wigan 24 Halifax 12 (Regal Trophy Final)|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-date=14 July 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140714164411/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2840|url-status=dead}} ===Auckland Warriors=== Iro was signed to play in the [[1998 Auckland Warriors season]]. He scored two tries in New Zealand's [[1998 ANZAC Test]] victory over Australia. ===St Helens=== He was St Helens' lone try-scorer in their [[1999 Super League Grand Final]] victory over [[Bradford Bulls]]. Having won the 1999 Championship, [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] contested the [[2000 World Club Challenge]] against [[National Rugby League]] Premiers the [[Melbourne Storm]], with Iro playing at {{rlp|CE}} in the loss. Iro played for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] at {{rlp|CE}} in their [[2000 Super League Grand Final]] victory over [[Wigan Warriors]]. As [[Super League V]] champions, St Helens played against [[2000 NRL season|2000 NRL]] Premiers, the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in the [[2001 World Club Challenge]]. Iro played at centre in St. Helens' victory. ==Coaching career== In 2006 Iro coached the [[Cook Islands national rugby league team|Cook Islands national team]] in a three-match series against the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]]. In the third and deciding match Iro came out of retirement, scoring two tries in a 32–4 victory.{{cite book | last =John Coffey, Bernie Wood | title = 100 years: Māori rugby league, 1908-2008 | publisher =Huia Publishers | year = 2008 | pages = 338 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=nklWo8vw-iIC | isbn = 9781869693312}} He also represented the Cook Islands at the [[2006 Commonwealth Games]] playing [[Rugby sevens at the 2006 Commonwealth Games|rugby sevens]]. ==References== {{reflist|40em}} ==External links== * [http://wigan.rlfans.com/readarticle.php?article_id=568 Statistics at
